channel.title: R on Colin Bousige
channel.description: Recent content in R on Colin Bousige

item.title: Introduction to R for data treatment and plotting
item.description: This is a short introduction to the R language and its application to data treatment for physico-chemists. This tutorial was mainly written for internal use (i.e. for reference for my students), so it is probably lacking a lot of explanation… I’ll complete it when I find time. Go here for the post with floating TOC (easier to navigate) Table of contents Getting ready Beginners Advanced Variables Scalars and booleans Vectors Different ways of defining a vector Principal operations on vectors Accessing values Sorting Maximum and minimum Mathematical operations Statistics on vectors More stuff Matrices and data frames Matrices Defining a data frame Accessing values Adding columns or rows Lists Functions Libraries Install libraries Load libraries Plotting Base graphics Basic plotting Adding some style Stacking plots Barplots and densities Image plots Advanced plotting using ggplot2 Making interactive plots with ggplot2 and plotly Fitting Linear fitting Base graphics ggplot2 Non linear fitting Example of data treatment with interactive plotting

item.title: Switching from $\LaTeX$ to Rmarkdown for all-purpose academics-related writing
item.description: Setting up your system Writing in markdown and exporting to various formats Like many physicists, I have been using \(\LaTeX\) since the very beginning of my undergrad studies, because it’s just that great. The main interests are that the content is dissociated from the form (so you can focus on your writing), it handles your references to articles, sections, figures and equations, and it just makes very nice documents.

item.title: My first attempt at a Shiny app
item.description: This is my first try as a Shiny app, I made it to help my students during their high pressure experiments using diamond anvil cell. It computes: The pressure if you give it values of \(\lambda_{laser}\), the R1 Raman shift at 0 pressure \(\omega_0\), and the measured R1 Raman shift \(\omega_{ruby}\) The expected Raman shift for the ruby, \(\omega_{ruby}\), if you give it the other info The pressure if you upload a ruby spectrum and give it the other info To use it:

item.title: Plotting 3D data with R
item.description: In my work I do a lot of measurements that I like to plot as a color map, like the evolution of a Raman spectrum as a function of temperature, pressure or position. In some cases I end up with a 3-columns data.frame with x, y, and z values (e.g. intensity of a peak as a function of the position on my sample), in some cases I have a list of spectra evolving with a given parameter.
